label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, so I need to analyze this ethical dilemma about whether to tip a waiter who provided poor service. Let me start by breaking down the problem. The user is at a restaurant with bad service but societal norms expect a tip. The question is whether to follow the norm or not tip because the service was bad. First, I should consider different ethical theories. Utilitarianism would look at the greatest good for the most people. If not tipping would set a bad precedent and hurt the restaurant's employees, maybe it's better to tip. But if tipping despite poor service doesn't actually improve the service, maybe it's not useful. Deontological ethics is about duty and rules. If the rule is to always tip, then following it is a duty regardless of the service. But if the rule is about fair pay based on service quality, then poor service might justify not tipping. Virtue ethics would consider the character of the person. Being a good, kind person might mean tipping to support someone in need, even if the service was bad. But being fair might
